metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

coverage.verify_error: step 'review@then/verify_branch@then/verify' failed: the model hit the token cap before finishing (finish_reason=length) — its output is TRUNCATED; raise max_tokens or split the step

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit fallback AC (or sub-bullets within AC1) that specifies the ref->tiny-commit design (blob schema equivalence, CAS semantics, CI token scope) so the executor can implement and validate either path without stopping to ask.
findings.finding: The plan defers the real design decision of whether to use ref->blob or ref->tiny-commit to the executor ('verified FIRST (C1 AC0)'), and all Acceptance Criteria are written only for the blob path — if GitHub rejects blob-backed refs, the ACs as written are incorrect and the executor has no specced alternative to implement.
findings.location: Design section — 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven' paragraph; AC list (all ACs assume blob path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC1: 'If GitHub rejects bare blob refs, the ref instead points to a minimal synthetic commit whose tree contains only the JSON blob; all CAS and schema requirements below apply identically to both variants.' Then mark C1 AC0 as a go/no-go gate that selects between the two sub-ACs rather than leaving the fallback unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the migration mechanism for already-initialized trackers in AC5 (e.g., 'a one-shot `rebar init --repair` idempotent command that patches `.gitattributes` in-place and force-pushes only that file to `origin/tickets`') and add a corresponding integration or fixture test that asserts a pre-migration `.gitattributes` is correctly updated.
findings.finding: AC5 requires 'a migration that also updates already-initialized trackers' to remove `.reconciler-*` from `.gitattributes`, but specifies no observable completion criterion, no mechanism (script, force-push, idempotent init re-run), and no test that the migration was applied — making this sub-requirement both ambiguous (E2) and unmeasurable (F1).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5: '`.reconciler-*` removed from the `merge=ours` `.gitattributes` ... via a migration that also updates already-initialized trackers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a gated sub-task in AC1/AC4) that explicitly requires the C1 AC0 GitHub feasibility probe to be completed and its outcome recorded (pass → proceed with ref→blob; fail → trigger ref→tiny-commit contingency) before any other ACs are worked.
findings.finding: The Design explicitly designates a C1 AC0 feasibility verification — "whether GitHub accepts push/fetch of a non-branch `refs/reconciler/*` ref pointing at a blob (with the CI `GITHUB_TOKEN`) is verified FIRST (C1 AC0)" — as a gated prerequisite for the entire epic, but no Acceptance Criterion covers this verification step or requires its outcome to be documented/recorded.
findings.location: Design section ('Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven') vs Acceptance Criteria (all 7 ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Insert as AC0: '[ ] GitHub feasibility probe: a standalone script pushes `refs/reconciler/probe` pointing at a blob via GITHUB_TOKEN and fetches it back; result is recorded in the ADR created by AC4 and determines whether the ref→blob or ref→commit path is used for all subsequent ACs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit verification point (in AC1 or AC7) that `_is_cas_mismatch` (or its replacement) accepts an arbitrary ref name argument, not just `refs/heads/tickets`, and that the CAS retry loop correctly retries on contention for `refs/reconciler/*` refs.
findings.finding: The Design explicitly states that `_is_cas_mismatch` at `_advisory_lock.py:415` line 425 'hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets` and must be generalized to accept the ref name' — a required implementation change — but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this generalization was performed.
findings.location: Design section ('Reuse the existing CAS retry helper... its discriminator _is_cas_mismatch... hard-codes refs/heads/tickets and must be generalized') vs Acceptance Criteria AC1 and AC7
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to read: '...the existing ref-CAS primitive is reused with `_is_cas_mismatch` generalized to accept any ref name (regression: CAS conflict on `refs/reconciler/lock` is retried, not raised as a hard error).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the migration path for existing `lock_max_retries` config entries (e.g., emit a deprecation warning for one release before removal, or explicitly state the validator ignores/errors on unknown keys and document the replacement mapping `lock_max_retries` → `lock_lease_secs`).
findings.finding: The removal of config key `lock_max_retries` (and its env aliases) is a breaking change for existing operator configs, but the plan specifies no migration semantics: will the schema validator error on the unknown key, silently ignore it, or emit a deprecation warning before removal? Without a stated migration path for already-deployed config files, operators on the file-backend path who roll back will have a broken config.
findings.location: AC6 / Design `lock_lease_secs` / `_config_schema.py:334,445`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-version deprecation step: if `lock_max_retries` is present in the config, log a WARNING naming the replacement `lock_lease_secs`, then proceed normally; hard-remove the key in the following version.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a reverse migration for the gitattributes change: when rolling back to `lock_backend=file`, the `_commands/init.py` init path (or a named migration step) must re-add the `merge=ours` rule for `.reconciler-*` to already-initialized trackers.
findings.finding: The forward migration for already-initialized trackers (updating their gitattributes to remove `.reconciler-*` from `merge=ours`) is mentioned, but the reverse migration — re-adding the `merge=ours` rule if an operator rolls back to `lock_backend=file` — is not described. The `lock_backend=file` switch alone does not restore the protective gitattributes entry, leaving the file-backend in a degraded state during rollback.
findings.location: AC5 / Design `Expand-contract cutover` / `_commands/init.py:57`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit and update all user-facing documentation (README, operator guide, config reference, CHANGELOG) to remove or replace references to `lock_max_retries` and document the replacement `lock_lease_secs` before the removal lands.
findings.finding: The plan removes `lock_max_retries` and its env aliases from the schema but does not mention auditing or updating any user-facing documentation (README, operator guide, config reference) that may reference these config keys, risking stale references in docs after removal.
findings.location: AC6 / `_config_schema.py:334,445`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the heartbeat thread distinguishes a transient push error (network timeout, 503) from a genuine CAS mismatch (ref moved by a contender), and add at least a short retry/back-off for transient errors before setting lock_lost — and plan a probe or unit-level fault injection to validate this path.
findings.finding: The heartbeat push path has no specified retry logic for transient network failures. A momentary push failure during a long pass causes the heartbeat daemon to surface a CAS failure, sets lock_lost, and aborts the pass — even though no contender stole the lock. This assumption (that every push failure means 'lease lost/stolen') is neither validated by the planned probe nor distinguished from genuine theft.
findings.location: Design section — 'Lease + heartbeat' paragraph; AC line 3 (holder renews at ~lease/3 from daemon thread)
findings.suggested_fix: In the _ref_lock.py design, differentiate the git push exit code / stderr for a transport error vs. a rejected CAS update (git prints 'rejected ... (fetch first)' or similar). Apply ≤3 retries with exponential back-off for transport errors before declaring lease lost. Add a fault-injection unit test that simulates a push returning exit-1 without a CAS-mismatch stderr pattern and asserts lock_lost is NOT set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lock_lost signaling primitive (e.g. threading.Event) and the checkpoint protocol (which __main__.py call sites poll it, at what granularity) in the design or AC, and ensure the new ADR or _ref_lock.py docstring documents this cross-component contract.
findings.finding: The inter-thread signaling mechanism between the heartbeat daemon thread and the reconciler pass execution thread (the 'lock_lost flag') is unspecified. Without naming the synchronization primitive (e.g. threading.Event, a guarded boolean, a queue), implementers have no contract to code to, and there is no guarantee the flag is visible across threads without explicit memory ordering.
findings.location: Design section — 'Lease + heartbeat' paragraph; AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Use threading.Event as the lock_lost signal (thread-safe, waitable, clearly named). Define a list of named checkpoints in __main__.py where the event is checked (e.g. after each Jira batch, before each write phase). Document this in the ADR alongside the daemon-thread lifecycle (daemon=True so it auto-dies on process exit).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the required deploy ordering (or atomic config push) so that all lock users — local clone(s) AND the GA bridge — flip `lock_backend` simultaneously, and document the safe coexistence window (or confirm it is zero by design).
findings.finding: Deploy ordering during the file→ref cutover is unspecified: if the local clone and the GitHub-Actions reconcile bridge flip the `lock_backend` config at different times, one side will hold the file-based lock while the other acquires the ref-based lock, providing ZERO mutual exclusion for the entire coexistence window.
findings.location: Design section — 'Expand-contract cutover' paragraph; AC4 (lock_backend switch)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or ADR section that states: 'Both the local clone and the GA-bridge runner must have `lock_backend = ref` set before either is started on the new code; a mixed-backend deployment is unsupported. The recommended cutover is a single commit to the shared config repo (or GitHub Variable update) followed by restarting all runners before the next scheduled pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or integration-test scenario that exercises the ref→file rollback path (flip config, confirm file-backend lock acquire/release works, confirm no leftover refs interfere with a subsequent re-enable of ref-backend).
findings.finding: The rollback path (flipping `lock_backend` back to `file`) is described in prose and captured in an ADR, but no AC requires a *tested* rollback — i.e. a test or runbook step that exercises the switch from ref-backend to file-backend and confirms locking resumes correctly without data cleanup.
findings.location: Design — 'Expand-contract cutover'; AC4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the explicit fetch refspec a contender MUST run before beginning the 'observe for one lease' window (e.g., `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock`) and confirm this fetch is part of the acquire/check protocol in `_ref_lock.py`.
findings.finding: The mechanism for a contender to read the authoritative lock state from origin is unspecified: standard `git fetch origin` uses the default refspec `+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*` and does NOT fetch `refs/reconciler/*`. A contender that has not explicitly fetched `refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` from origin will observe a stale (or absent) local ref, compute fence=0/oid=zero, wait one full lease on its own clock, re-read the same stale local ref, and then falsely conclude the holder made no progress — triggering a CAS-break against a live holder (false steal).
findings.location: Design section — 'Relative-duration, skew-proof lease' + 'Blob creation' paragraphs; AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: In the acquire/check flow, before reading the local refs/reconciler/lock, always run `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/lock:refs/reconciler/lock` (or equivalent `git ls-remote` + explicit update). Document this as a required step in the AC and in the _ref_lock.py module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the expand-contract cutover maintains mutual exclusion during the mixed-backend window — either require all clones to flip atomically (e.g. via a config commit the reconciler reads on startup), or have the ref backend also check-then-acquire the file lock transiently during the overlap period.
findings.finding: During the expand-contract cutover window, a clone running `lock_backend=file` and a clone running `lock_backend=ref` operate in completely disjoint coordination namespaces: the file-backend clone writes `.reconciler-pass-lock` to the working tree; the ref-backend clone reads/writes `refs/reconciler/lock`. Neither can see the other's lock. Both clones will simultaneously believe they hold the exclusive lock, meaning the mixed-backend window has ZERO concurrency protection — a worse outcome than either backend alone.
findings.location: Design section — 'Expand-contract cutover' paragraph; AC #4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the gc impact of heartbeat-renewal blobs (each renewal leaves a prior blob unreachable) and specify whether `_ref_lock.py` will explicitly delete (`git update-ref -d`) prior blobs or rely on standard gc, so operators can tune gc.auto if needed.
findings.finding: Each heartbeat renewal writes a new blob via `git hash-object -w` then moves the ref, leaving the prior blob unreachable immediately. The plan specifies no gc discipline for these short-lived unreachable blobs. For a 60-minute pass with ~lease/3 = 40s heartbeat, that is ~90 unreachable loose blobs per pass. The repo's gc.autoDetach=true but default gc.auto threshold is 6700 loose objects, so blobs won't trigger auto-gc on their own — they accumulate until the next manual or scheduled gc.
findings.location: Design section — 'Lease + heartbeat' paragraph; AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: On release, explicitly delete the lock ref with `git update-ref -d refs/reconciler/lock <old-oid>` (already implied but not stated) so the blob is unreachable from the moment of release. For in-flight renewals, the plan could note that prior blobs are unreachable and rely on standard gc, or run `git prune` as part of a periodic maintenance step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion that `refs/reconciler/*` pointing at a blob SHA propagates correctly via `git push`/`git fetch` on GitHub and is GC-safe before committing to the ref→blob design (the plan's stated contingency — ref→tiny-commit — keeps standard git reachability and should be pre-evaluated against the blob approach).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a git ref pointing directly at a blob SHA (not a commit) will reliably propagate across `git push`/`git fetch` to GitHub and remain GC-safe — but this is an unverified capability assertion. Standard git pack-protocol advertises refs by the reachability graph rooted at commits; a ref→blob sits outside that graph and may not transfer as a reachable object, and GitHub's GC may prune loose blobs not reachable from any commit. The plan's own wording ('Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven') acknowledges the uncertainty but frames it only as an operational note, not as a blocking prerequisite AC.
findings.location: Design section — 'Blob creation: git hash-object -w --stdin … then a CAS on the ref' and 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven'
findings.suggested_fix: Promote C1 AC0 from prose note to a concrete gated verification step: write a standalone spike script that pushes a blob-pointed ref to the actual GitHub remote with GITHUB_TOKEN, fetches it from a second clone, verifies the blob is present and unfragmented, then checks after a GC run. If this fails, fall back immediately to the ref→lightweight-commit contingency (which uses standard git object reachability and has no GC risk).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification (in the ADR required by AC4) explaining how the DynamoDB-lock-client pattern's assumptions map to a git-ref-backed blob lock (specifically: where TTL-at-storage is replaced by application-side expiry, how transient heartbeat-push failures differ from genuine CAS-loss, and why simpler alternatives like ref→commit+expiry-field were rejected).
findings.finding: The plan invokes the 'DynamoDB-lock-client pattern' by name as the justification for its relative-duration lease design, but does not demonstrate that this pattern's underlying assumptions carry over to a git-ref-backed lock, nor that established alternatives (e.g. ref→lightweight-commit with an expiry field, Redis SETNX+expiry, or a simpler single-heartbeat approach) were considered and rejected. The DynamoDB lock client relies on DynamoDB's native conditional writes and TTL-at-storage-layer; a git-ref blob has neither — the expiry check is entirely application-side, which is a material adaptation the plan does not analyse.
findings.location: Design section — 'Relative-duration, skew-proof lease (DynamoDB-lock-client pattern)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an update-in-place `.gitattributes` migration (not just create-if-absent) that removes the `.reconciler-*` line from already-committed `.gitattributes` files on already-initialized tracker branches, and confirm the new migration path is idempotent (safe to re-run).
findings.finding: The `.gitattributes` migration for already-initialized trackers has no update-in-place mechanism: `_commit_gitattributes` (init.py:414) is create-if-absent — it only commits `.gitattributes` when the file does NOT already exist, so it will silently skip every already-initialized tracker that has the file, leaving `.reconciler-* merge=ours` in place on all existing branches.
findings.location: AC: `.reconciler-* removed from … merge=ours .gitattributes (_commands/init.py:57, via a migration that also updates already-initialized trackers)`; `src/rebar/_commands/init.py:410-425`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_migrate_gitattributes_remove_reconciler(tracker)` function that reads the current committed `.gitattributes`, strips the `.reconciler-* merge=ours` line, and commits the updated version if changed (idempotent: no-op if the line is already absent). Call it from the existing-tracker path in `init` (init.py:207) alongside the existing `_commit_gitattributes` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the `.gitattributes` update (removing `.reconciler-* merge=ours`) must be staged to occur at C4 (file backend removal), not at C3 (ref backend introduction), so that the rollback window `lock_backend=file` remains safe with merge=ours intact.
findings.finding: Removing `.reconciler-* merge=ours` from `.gitattributes` is an irreversible committed change that is NOT rolled back by flipping `lock_backend` back to `file`: if the ref-backend is faulty and operators revert the config switch, file-based lock files on diverged histories will now conflict (no `merge=ours` protection) instead of resolving silently — exactly the breakage the original policy existed to prevent.
findings.location: Design section: `lock_backend = file | ref switch enables an expand-contract cutover`; AC: `lock_backend = file | ref switch enables an expand-contract cutover; … rollback path`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit migration step that `git rm --cached` any existing `.reconciler-*` files from the tickets branch working tree on already-initialized trackers, with the cleanup being idempotent (no-op if the files are already absent).
findings.finding: Stale `.reconciler-*` files already committed on existing tickets branches are not cleaned up as part of the migration: the plan says 'no `.reconciler-*` file is committed to the tickets branch (regression test)' but describes no `git rm` + commit step for pre-existing committed lock files on already-live tracker branches, leaving them as dangling tree entries after the `.gitattributes` protection is removed.
findings.location: AC: `no .reconciler-* file is committed to the tickets branch (regression test)`; Design: `Move pass-lock and phase-gate OFF the working tree`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the Design and in AC#1 the exact fetch refspec (`git fetch origin '+refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*'`) that all clones must run before reading the lock, and add it to the GHA workflow's Mount-tickets-branch step.
findings.finding: `refs/reconciler/*` is not in any fetch refspec, so contender clones will not automatically see the authoritative lock state from origin; a `git fetch origin refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*` (or equivalent configured refspec) is required but never stated.
findings.location: Design section — 'Blob creation' and 'Relative-duration, skew-proof lease'; AC#1
findings.suggested_fix: Add `git fetch origin '+refs/reconciler/*:refs/reconciler/*'` (or a configured refspec in `.git/config`) to the reconciler startup and to the GHA workflow before the reconciler step; document this precondition in AC#1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a conditional clause to AC#1 and AC#2 (e.g. 'blob OR tiny-commit if C1 AC0 finds GitHub rejects blob refs; the chosen wire format is recorded in the ADR') so the ACs remain valid under both outcomes of the feasibility spike.
findings.finding: AC#1 and AC#2 are written entirely in blob-semantics terms, but the plan's own contingency (ref → tiny-commit if GitHub rejects blob refs) invalidates their wording; there is no stated re-write path for the ACs if C1 AC0 forces the contingency.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#1 ('bare ref -> blob'), AC#2 ('Blob is UTF-8 JSON'); Design — 'Remote feasibility is load-bearing and unproven (C1 AC0)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Design and AC#3 the auth precondition for daemon-thread heartbeat pushes (e.g. 'git remote origin must have push credentials available in the git credential store, as provided by `persist-credentials: true` in the GHA workflow and by the operator's git config in local runs').
findings.finding: Auth preconditions for the heartbeat daemon thread's periodic `git push` to origin are not stated; the plan relies on implicit GHA `persist-credentials: true` behaviour that is not documented as a required precondition.
findings.location: Design — 'Lease + heartbeat: … holder renews from a daemon thread started on acquire; a renewal-CAS failure … aborts the pass'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the minimum `GITHUB_TOKEN` permission scope required by the new `refs/reconciler/*` push/fetch surface (expected: `contents: write` only, no additional scopes) in both the ADR and the CI workflow that enables `lock_backend = ref`, so the token is correctly scoped before the ref backend is activated (AC4 / Design 'expand-contract cutover').
findings.finding: The plan introduces periodic authenticated pushes to `refs/reconciler/*` on the GitHub remote (heartbeat renewals + acquire/release) using `GITHUB_TOKEN`, but never declares the minimum required token permission scope for this new push surface.
findings.location: Design section — 'Authoritative on origin (the sync.remote)' + 'whether GitHub accepts push/fetch … with the CI GITHUB_TOKEN is verified FIRST (C1 AC0)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new ADR and the GitHub Actions workflow that runs the reconciler, add an explicit `permissions:` block: `contents: write` (for pushing `refs/reconciler/*`) and nothing else. Document this minimum in the `lock_backend = ref` onboarding notes so operators activating the ref backend know exactly what grant is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the inter-thread signalling primitive for `lock_lost` (e.g., `threading.Event`) and enumerate at least two named checkpoint sites in `__main__.py` / `reconcile.py` where the main-thread pass polls it before the abort.
findings.finding: The `lock_lost` inter-thread signalling mechanism is unspecified: the plan says a renewal-CAS failure 'sets a lock_lost flag and the pass aborts at its next checkpoint', but no synchronization primitive (threading.Event, atomic bool, etc.) is named, and 'checkpoint' has zero occurrences anywhere in the reconciler package — so the protocol decision rule is stated without the schema/API it needs.
findings.location: Design section — 'Lease + heartbeat' paragraph; AC3 ('a lost-lease renewal aborts the pass')
findings.suggested_fix: Use `threading.Event` (set by daemon, polled by main thread): name it `_lease_lost_event`; list explicit polling checkpoints, e.g., before each major reconcile phase boundary in `run_pass()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a `RefLockHandle` (or equivalent) data structure in `_ref_lock.py` that the `acquire` call returns and the daemon thread holds, carrying at minimum `(ref_oid: str, fence: int, lease_secs: float)` so heartbeat renewals CAS against the exact previously-written state.
findings.finding: The `fence` counter's per-renewal storage location in the daemon thread is ambiguous: the plan says `fence` is 'bumped on every acquire/renew/steal' but the blob is immutable JSON rewritten via CAS — the daemon thread must hold the current fence value somewhere between heartbeats, yet no in-memory state structure for the lock handle (class, namedtuple, etc.) is defined.
findings.location: Design section — 'Blob creation' and 'Lease + heartbeat' paragraphs; AC2 (blob schema); AC3 (holder renews at ~lease/3 from a daemon thread)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact generalization of `_is_cas_mismatch` — either add a `ref_name` parameter and thread it through `_cas_advance_with_retry`, or extract a general `'update-ref' in args and exc.returncode == 128` check — and enumerate all existing call sites that must be updated.
findings.finding: The `_is_cas_mismatch` discriminator hard-codes `'refs/heads/tickets' in args` (verified at `_advisory_lock.py:425`), but the plan says to 'generalize [it] to accept the ref name' without specifying the new decision rule — i.e., whether the generalized check accepts any `update-ref` invocation, checks for any member of a known-refs set, or takes a runtime parameter.
findings.location: _advisory_lock.py:425; Design section — 'Blob creation' paragraph ('_is_cas_mismatch … must be generalized to accept the ref name')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `ref_name: str` parameter to `_is_cas_mismatch` (default `'refs/heads/tickets'` for backward compatibility) and pass it through `_cas_advance_with_retry`; update the single existing call site in `_advisory_lock.py` to pass `'refs/heads/tickets'` explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign the `_config_schema.py` addition of `lock_lease_secs` explicitly to C2 (7522) AC4, with the same specificity as C3's assignment of `lock_backend`.
findings.finding: C2 (7522) introduces `lock_lease_secs` as a new [reconciler] config key but its acceptance criteria never explicitly assign the `_config_schema.py` entry to C2; C3 (2711) reads `lock_lease_secs` from config (daemon heartbeat cadence) assuming it is already schema-registered, leaving the schema addition potentially unowned.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C2 (7522) AC4: 'lock_lease_secs is added to _config_schema.py under [reconciler] (int, default 120, replaces the later-removed lock_max_retries); the config is read in _ref_lock.py constructor/renew cadence logic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in C1 (524d) AC2 that `acquire()` returns a handle/dataclass (e.g., `LockHandle(ref, oid, fence)`) that renew() and release() accept as their first argument.
findings.finding: C1 (524d) does not specify the return type / interface contract of `acquire()`, yet C2 (7522) requires the acquired OID to implement `renew()` (whose CAS uses 'old = observed oid'), and C3 (2711) explicitly says to 'thread the acquire handle/oid through acquire → renew → release' — creating an interface gap between C1's delivery and C2/C3's consumption.
findings.location: child 524d-00fa-66ef-4432 — AC2; child 7522-129b-1380-418c — AC1; child 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 — What (threading handle)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C1 AC2: 'acquire() returns a LockHandle(ref: str, oid: str, fence: int) on success; renew() and release() accept a LockHandle as input and return an updated LockHandle or raise on CAS failure.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in C1 (524d) AC3 that fence is initialized to 1 on first acquire (and on steal), so that 'fence unchanged across one lease' is unambiguous and never falsy.
findings.finding: `fence` initialization value on first `acquire()` and on `steal()` is unspecified in any child's acceptance criteria, even though the parent's self-heal correctness depends on the fence being a reliable progress witness bumped on every acquire/renew/steal.
findings.location: child 524d-00fa-66ef-4432 — AC3 (blob schema); child 7522-129b-1380-418c — AC1 (renew fence += 1), AC2 (expiry check 'fence unchanged')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to C1 AC3 and C2 AC1: 'fence is initialized to 1 on acquire/steal (never 0); renew increments fence by 1; the expiry check compares fence values numerically, not as a truthiness test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add gate-specific unit tests to C3 (2711) AC5: gate creation, atomic phase-transition CAS, and consumer 'treat unreadable as held' behavior (mirroring the lock tests but for refs/reconciler/gate).
findings.finding: C3 (2711) specifies the `refs/reconciler/gate` lifecycle as fully CAS-managed (creation, blob format, atomic phase transition, consumer behavior) but no sibling includes gate-specific unit tests; the only tests called out across all children target the lock ref (`refs/reconciler/lock`) via existing `test_advisory_lock*.py` extensions.
findings.location: child 2711-21bf-ab71-4136 — AC2, AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Extend C3 AC5 to: 'Gate-specific tests cover: gate creation (first-write CAS), atomic phase-transition CAS success and conflict, consumer fail-closed on corrupt gate blob; added to test_advisory_lock.py or a dedicated test_advisory_gate.py.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
